answer:
**Charles Comte's *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, chapter 9 ("De l'utilité et de la valeur primitives des fonds de terre [ 27 ]") argues that land value rises with civilization's progress, drawing on Alexander von Humboldt's report (*Political Essay on New Spain*) to tabulate population densities per square league across Mexican intendancies: Sonora (6), etc., up to Guanaxuato (586).**[^1]

The key line giving the numerical sequence states: "we will find that the value of lands increases, from one intendancy to another, in the following progression: **6, 7, 10, 12, 38, 65, 66, 81, 109, 120, 255, 301, 586**." It immediately continues: "The extent of land that would be worth only **six francs** in the former intendancy of **Sonora** would be worth two hundred and fifty-five in that of **Mexico**, and **five hundred and eighty-six** in that of **Guanaxuato**."[^1]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/09-de-lutilite-et-de-la-valeur-primitives-des-fonds-d/translation/#p-0fd6d5).
